name: Phoenix Lights Full
description: The Phoenix Lights refers to a series of widely sighted aerial phenomena observed in the skies over Arizona, Nevada, and Sonora, Mexico, on March 13, 1997. Proponents claim the event involved two distinct formations: a triangular or V-shaped formation of lights moving silently and slowly over the state, and a separate, stationary set of lights seen near Phoenix. The most common explanation offered by believers is that the lights represented a single, massive, unidentified flying object (UFO). The official explanation, provided by the U.S. Air Force, attributes the stationary lights to flares dropped during a training exercise by Maryland Air National Guard planes at Luke Air Force Base. However, many witnesses dispute this explanation, citing inconsistencies between the observed phenomena and the characteristics of flares. Skeptics also suggest misidentification of stars, planets (particularly Venus), and other conventional aircraft. The event gained significant media attention and remains a subject of debate and speculation, fueled by numerous witness accounts and the lack of a universally accepted explanation. The sheer number of credible witnesses, including a former Arizona governor, Fife Symington, who initially mocked the event but later claimed to have seen a similar object, contributes to the enduring interest in the Phoenix Lights. Critics point to the lack of conclusive physical evidence (such as radar data corroborating a large, unknown object) and the possibility of mass hysteria or misinterpretation of events.
subtitle: Mass Sighting of Unidentified Aerial Phenomena

claims.claim: A massive, solid UFO flew over Phoenix.
claims.evidence: Numerous eyewitness accounts describing a large, silent, V-shaped object with distinct lights.
claims.criticism: Lack of corroborating radar data; witness accounts are subjective and prone to misinterpretation.

claims.claim: The official explanation of flares is inadequate.
claims.evidence: Witnesses claim the lights were too large, too slow, and too silent to be flares; discrepancy in timing and location of the flares.
claims.criticism: Flares can appear larger and brighter depending on distance and atmospheric conditions; perception of speed and sound can be subjective.

claims.claim: The military is covering up the true nature of the event.
claims.evidence: Some believe the government is withholding information about UFOs and extraterrestrial contact.
claims.criticism: No concrete evidence of a cover-up; the Air Force explanation, while disputed, is plausible.

keyFigures.name: Fife Symington
keyFigures.role: Former Arizona Governor, initially skeptical, later claimed to witness a similar object.

keyFigures.name: James McGaha
keyFigures.role: Retired Air Force pilot and astronomer, proponent of the flare explanation.

mythologyConnections: Possible interpretations as modern-day dragons or celestial phenomena
Connections to ancient sky god beliefs

timeline.event: U.S. Air Force issues report identifying the lights as flares.
Fife Symington publicly states he saw a UFO during the event.

sources.author: Lynne Kitei
sources.work: The Phoenix Lights: A Skeptic's Discovery That We Are Not Alone
sources.title: Lynne Kitei
